comments.title: Planetary Charity
comments.content:
I am involved in the care of an elderly relative with dementia. She’s in hospital at the moment but it seems that there’s little more that can be done to help and the plan is to bring her home for end of life care. All the conventional matters will be taken care of, but if there’s anything else that could be done to make things easier for her I would very much like to do it.

I’ve had some success over the last few years with planetary charity, and I thought perhaps a petition to Saturn to ease her passage might help.

Unfortunately there is no way to gain her consent. She only has the vaguest idea about what is happening to her which is a blessing, but even if I could explain it in a way that she could understand, moving on to the concept of planetary charity seems well beyond the boundaries of the possible.

I know that this would normally end matters there but as it happens I recently became her legal guardian in matters concerning any health or welfare decisions. The paperwork was filed some time back during a period when she could understand and consent to me doing that for her when the time came. So in effect I now have the same legal relationship with her as that of the parent of a toddler. This is not just a mere legality, I have had to make serious life changing decisions on her behalf, based on by best understanding of her wishes.

Do you think that this is sufficient for me to undertake the ritual anyway? If so, would Saturn appropriate?

Andy

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity
replies.content: Since she gave you consent to make important decisions for her, and she herself is currently unable to give consent, if on reflection you feel it's appropriate to perform planetary charity on her behalf, it would be reasonable to do so. Yes, Saturn's the appropriate planetary power to invoke -- he rules everything having to do with old age and death.

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity
replies.content:
Thank you for this. A little more reflection on my part will do no harm, and then perhaps action.

Andy

comments.content:
Good evening!

Thank you very much for this weekly venue and the community you have carefully built around it. It has been tremendously spiritually valuable for me.

As for the questions, as I often have, here's something of a grab-bag, and I welcome any thoughts from the commentariat as well.

1) For meditation themes, what is your approach to balancing between following a schedule you have set ahead of time (e.g. "I will meditate on each aicme of the Ogham for 3 days") and meditating on potential themes that seem to present themselves (e.g. a seemingly portentous dream, a puzzling divination reading, a striking thought from your reading, etc.)?

2) You mentioned in a previous MM that the mind can tell apart products of visual imagination brought on by reading or listening from reality, but it doesn't truly make that distinction with visual images. That made me wonder: in a world with much less visual media (meaning, most of human history), were the few (usually religious) visual images likely to have a much more potent effect and to be felt as more consciously real? For some reason, the story that comes to mind is the Christian who viewed the ceremony of the Eleusinian mysteries and basically went "what, it's just a sheaf of wheat?" I don't think that story has a particularly strong "saturation of visual media" component, but the idea that his preconditions to not take the ritual seriously meant it didn't affect him strongly struck me as potentially related.

3) Related to the above: any thoughts on how the mind does with telling apart "products of imagination" versus "images seen with the eyes" when those images are the product of your imagination (such as a drawing you did yourself)?

4) Astrology has taken something of a backseat in my magical development to date, and I suspect it will continue to do so as I work through the DMH and its symbolism. Are there any Pareto Principle-type pieces of knowledge/behaviors (e.g. the 20% of behaviors responsible for the 80% of results) that anyone working on magic ought to know even if not learning astrological magic? I'm thinking something like "okay, you should at least make sure to avoid doing any rituals on the day of such-and-such or the hour of so-an-so". If the answer is "nope, you'll actually have to learn it if you want to avoid the pitfalls and reap the benefits", well, there's something to look forward to learning down the road!

5) I assume the answer to this is "prayer and meditation to find out what's up", but just in case you or any of the commentariat have further thoughts, something odd that's been happening to me: for the last several weeks, I have run especially into delays and other travel hindrances on Wednesdays (accidents blocking traffic, construction blocking lanes, trains stopped at crossings, other drivers going slow/acting confused/cutting me off). Now, some of this is surely due to the fact that I currently only go to the office on MWF, so Wednesday is one of the days where I can encounter such things at all. On the other hand, the fact that Wednesday is the day of Woden, who is a traveler and equated with Mercury in the Interpretatio Romana, and that Woden is a God whom I especially worship, makes me stop and wonder "what is he trying to tell me?" So, other than appropriate offerings, prayers for guidance, and meditating on the symbolism of traveling, delays, and blockages, any other thoughts on what this might mean or what I should do?

6) I had a dream where I was surprised that my wife said something about "Hu the Mighty", who I went on to explain was a Druid Revival God. Hu Himself did not show up in the dream, other than as a topic of discussion. To date, I have not prayed to Hu the Mighty or invoked him in my magic. Besides that I should meditate on it, any recommendations on what I might do or look into as a result of this dream?

Thanks very much,
Jeff

replies.content:

If I'm really certain I need to meditate about something else, I schedule a second session for that day, or for several days, as needed.

2) Yes, very much so.

3) It depends on how clear, intense, and focused your imagination is.

In re to Jeff's question #2, and I hope I'm not misunderstanding his question, what role would iconoclasm play in this as this is the rejection of images altogether? We see this in Islam of course as well as Protestantism and appears to be reaching a high level recently with the dynamiting of Buddhist images by the Taliban, Al-Qaida destruction of mausoleums in Mali and vandalism of Buddhist images in Asia mainly by Christian fundamentalists.

If as Jeff speculates visual images were more potent in ancient times, then would the current fanatical iconoclasm coupled with the super-saturation of current visual media not only reduce the hold of religious images on the imagination but perhaps weaken the religious beliefs themselves by making them too abstract? As someone who enjoys drawing, I find the loss of images deeply impoverishing from a creative standpoint but that's just my own reaction. Also, what would the occult effect be if any?

JLfromNH/Crimson Grimy Hippogriff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I tend to see iconoclasm as part of the revolt of the abstract mind against concrete lived experience -- an important factor in culture over the last two millennia or so. The ideologies of the word -- of verbal abstractions -- are particularly prone to it. The saturation of the environment with fictive images (TV, etc.) is another expression of the same thing: instead of seeing reality, stare at these images of the unreal!  Sacred images of the classic kind get part of their power from their embeddedness in lived reality -- that's why in so many traditions the images are deliberately not realistic. They symbolize rather than imitating, and so can be experienced as themselves rather than mistaken for what they represent.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Is this why it pops up so much in Abrahamic religions? Because these are 'religions of the Book' where people are encouraged to study their sacred literature rather than offer devotions to images. With the increase in literacy after the development of the printing press, the rise of iconoclastic Protestantism, Calvinism in particular, with their rejection of statues, icons, paintings, even card playing with their little images rose out of that. There seems to be a rejection of music as well (with its accompanying dancing and singing). Talk about squelching the human spirit!

It makes me think of some of the complaints I've seen here by meditators who seem unable to do visualizations for meditation and need to have work arounds as a result. I have no problems conjuring mental images and often have vivid dreams as well. Some of that might be due to my Catholic upbringing with its rich visual imagery as well as natural creativity. I can still recall from my early childhood the paintings which used to be on the walls and ceiling of the church I went to. I have a vague memory of a pelican on the ceiling over the altar but can't recall any details. Alas, all got plastered over as a result of the Vatican 2 spasms.

Lots of food for thought here!

JLfromNH/Crimson Grimy Hippogriff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, the religions of the Book have a particularly bad case of it. The Catholic and Orthodox ends of Christianity have done a fairly good job of avoiding that trap, though it's taken a lot of struggle. Protestantism -- well, it's worth remembering that the Ottoman Empire was nearing its zenith when the Reformation broke out. I've often thought that Protestantism is basically what happens when a bunch of Christians got a bad case of Islam envy and remodeled their faith accordingly.

replies.title: Re: An uncertain hallo from below
replies.content:
JMG, this issue seems to have come up a few times here. I read Josephine McCarthy's Quareia materials with great interest when she first published them, and I think that people who say she says not to banish are (willfully?) misreading her, probably mostly by skimming sections without reading the whole system.

It is certainly true that Josephine McCarthy is no fan of the traditional Golden Dawn banishing rituals, which if I recall rightly she compares to getting four aggressive bouncers to walk around you constantly glaring at anyone suspicious who comes too close, and sometimes booting spirits out of their own homes. After all, you may know some pretty bad people in the material world, but you don't go around trying to throw them out of their home whenever you come round. In a way, this is just a more extreme viewpoint on similar things you've said yourself regarding traditional GD banishing rituals and nature spirits.

However various magical protection rituals do, in fact, have a place in her system, including most prominently a directional ritual that is clearly her own system's version of a core protective and balancing ritual-- which builds in power over time, as the student ascends through the system.

Where it seems to me that McCarthy's philosophy probably differs from yours is that she believes there is a kind of natural spiritual immune system which doesn't get sufficiently strengthened for later heavy magical work if you learn powerful protection too early on in your magical training. This seems similar, in a way, to what I can glean of your reasoning when you advise not to overly protect children, except via natural magic; she seems to think it also applies to adults, if they wish to travel far on the path of the magician. So she advises "protection ritual when needed", not "protection ritual daily", which is not the same thing as "don't do protection rituals".

However right or wrong she may be in that contention that a daily protection ritual is unnecessary, she certainly takes major efforts to make up for it with an extremely strong emphasis on teaching and encouraging various forms of spiritual cleansing, balance, and yes, protection. In the course of her very first module, she teaches many forms of protection linked with prayer, including incense, bells, candles, chants, magically aided house cleaning, a simple protective talisman, and a spiritual cleansing bath ritual that she instructs the student to repeat once a week for several weeks on end before settling down to a "do when necessary" pace. Also in another early module is a lesson wherein the student does a deep cleanse and tidy every inch of their residence, and then uses divination skills to aid in rearranging their house in the most energetically beneficial way possible, along with some simple Feng Shui; and the understanding is that one cannot continue in the system until they actually do this.

Now, you may still not approve of her approach, of course. Certainly one obvious danger is that "perform when necessary" can quickly devolve into "never". And there are other parts of what she teaches that I am rather certain that you would disagree with. But it is worth knowing that there is something a bit more substantial behind the straw man that some seem to want to watch you attack.

replies.title: Re: An uncertain hallo from below
replies.content:
JMG, while Magic Monday has finished, I would appreciate it if you could put this through, as these conversations remain available for people to review at later dates.

Tunesmyth, thank you for articulating this so clearly and with a great level of detail. It's what I wish I'd had the ability to write at very late o'clock local time.

I would add one more thing about banishing - when I was an absolute beginner, I found daily banishing helpful in my personal circumstances. But as I have grown, I decided to adapt from McCarthy's work and see how I feel / how things went when stretching out a few days between banishing rituals. And it was absolutely a great learning experience. It helped me to learn to feel and understand my environment with a much greater level of specificity, and I am a better practitioner now as a result.

All that being the case - I do wholeheartedly agree with what JMG has said on other occasions: if one is following a training program as a beginner, it is very sensible to follow that training program exactly. McCarthy, from what I have analyzed (I also read the Quareia materials with great interest) has built in safety measures for students in the beginning stages of the work.

Not every magical training approach requires a formal ritual magic banishing to be safe and effective. There are many ways to climb the mountain, lets say.

However, for those training programs that include a LBRP or SOP style banishing, it is advisable that students, once started on the path, follow it until they gain enough strength to decide what other options they might want to explore. GD-style magic approaches can - sometimes if not generally - 'light' people up on the subtle planes in such a way that the banishing becomes crucial for quite some time. 'Time' being measured in possibly years, or the entirety of a lifetime and beyond.

Again, Tunesmyth, thank you for taking the time to eloquently communicate, and JMG, thank you for your patience on a non-Magic Monday Tuesday.

Mauve Abominable Troglodyte

replies.title: Re: An uncertain hallo from below
replies.content:
1. I agree with your critique regarding her stance on banishing in a general sense, but a close reading shows her position is more nuanced than “never banish” (she was a practicing exorcist for quite some time). Sometimes “big things” can feel scary, and banishing is not always the correct first step.

Also, I think it’s important to not assume that everything someone has to say is problematic or wrong or completely not worth reading because one position is inadvisable if taken to the extreme. One might explore more deeply and then come to the conclusion that it’s not a path they’d choose to follow, but part of learning is is learning how to figure out what is and is not helpful advice. I, personally, have found her books to be worth reading and thinking about, even though I’ve not yet ‘met’ an author I agree with on every point.

2. I have no particular stance on whether McCarthy is correct about the impact of Fortune’s war work on her health. I do however know from experience that any magical act can impact health, and therefore the risks with this kind of worth are worth thinking about - from an ‘informed consent’ perspective if nothing else.

Assuming that this isn't a medical problem and you have already been cleared by your physician to listen to music and engage in crack-pot pseudoscience:

It's not a popular method in western systems, but this is a thing. Different sounds have different effects on the subtle bodies. Put together well, they can have an intense effect on different parts of the listener or their surroundings. In Tibet, there is an entire orchestral tradition centered around drawing out mental imbalances so that the listener can deal with them. Anything over the internet is going to be wild supposition, but this sounds like destructive interference of the heart-center.

replies.title: Re: Musical response
replies.content:
IIRC, Gregorian chant has/had(?) whole guidebooks governing appropriate combinations of notes for various evocative purposes. As in... going from a particular note up to another particular note, or down to a particular note, corresponded with a very specific feeling, and in composing such chants, one would try to get the appropriate feeling lined up with the words. Penitence, reverence, joy, grief... there was a definite recognition, and exploitation of, the way that certain intervals affect the listener (and the chanter!).

I can say from experience that Eastern chant can be used to induce altered states of consciousness.

There is no reason modern music could not employ something similar. I wonder what the effect would be if you stumbled on some of the techniques, but used them without any awareness? It seems plausible that you could get unintentional and/or undesirable effects.

replies.title: Re: Musical response
replies.content:
I would assume that you are correct. I know for a fact that there are byzantine books on the subject and an eastern orthodox priest was actually the one who introduced me to it. They are definitely still using it, though I can't say how intensely.

As far as I can tell, the techniques are just as reliant on context as any other tool, though in this case the context is the rest of the composition not a specific ritual space. I think cluelessly chaining together sounds without an awareness of their spiritual effects results in astral (or etheric) noise. This could explain the soulless quality that afflicts so much of modern music.

replies.title: Re: Musical response
replies.content:
AFAIK Byzantine chant still very much uses that tradition in monasteries, and I assume in traditionally-Orthodox countries. But in the US at least it's a subject of confusion and sometimes acrimonious debate, because lining up the right tonal expressions with the correct parts of the text is a monumental task any time you translate it into a new language. English translations are a dreadful mishmash of sacrificing the word/phrase associations to keep the melody, sacrificing the melody in order to have a good translation, Sacrificing the poetry in order to have a literal translation... it's a mess. There are composers working on it, but it needs a lot more time to mature, and in the meantime those of us who speak only English are missing out on a lot of the... experience? praxis? benefit? I don't have the right word for it.

One curious thing I noticed after being a chanter for a few years, was that it became difficult to listen to a lot of pop music that I used to like. Like, it used to be catchy, and now it's like flies buzzing around my ears: just irritating. And that is for someone without formal training, who only speaks English, muddling around in a sea of cobbled-together translations. I can only imagine what the effect would be, of being immersed in the full experience.

comments.title: Some Qs
comments.content:
Hi JMG!

Thank you for your guidance; it’s an unbelievable gift that you’re willing to help students along the path who are working with your published instruction. It’s rare to be able to ask an author for clarification and it’s rare in life to be given straightforward and useful answers to questions in general.

On my mind this week:

1. I’ve been a fan of David Lynch since around 1991. I’ve been curious about his Transcendental Meditation practice since I first heard about it. I’m not a practitioner and don’t have any interest in it; I agree with you there’s enough in the classic Western tradition to render no real need to go looking elsewhere. Hearing him talk about TM, and having now some years background in occult practice, I find there seems to be some usefulness to TM, but my suspicion is that it’s a bunny-slope approach. His description of diving within to the Unified Field, which he equates with God, the Kingdom of Heaven, the true self, etc seems similar to cabalistic ideas, except the metaphors are reversed: diving rather than rising. His notion that the method is effortless goes against everything about spiritual practice I’ve experienced, whether discursive mediation, ritual magic, or Christian worship, all of which take a great deal of effort to produce any result whatsoever, in my experience. What are your thoughts about TM?
2. I’ve taken to heart your advice last week about your working around your published material. So much so that I happened upon a website that I thought would contain an interview with you, but I realized as I was reading it, that it was an excerpt from a book I’m aware you’ve published, but haven’t read or bought before. So I went and purchased the book. Even though I didn’t intentionally go there to steal anything, I felt it was better to err on the side of caution. My question is about samples. I’ve stopped reading any books I’ve downloaded from the internet that I could instead purchase, and only read books I haven’t paid for if I can be quite sure they’re in the public domain or are offered by someone in good faith like the Candle Magic pdf you posted recently. But I have samples of books I’ve downloaded from Amazon. I tend to download these samples they offer to help me make a decision whether to buy the book or not. I have, for example, a sample of The Celtic Golden Dawn, which is not a system I’m working with, but was curious about purchasing. What are your thoughts about these samples and how they tie in or don’t tie in to your working?
3. How would you do a divination before a working? What question would you ask and what would the spread be? Can you give an example?
4. If a friend asks you to housesit and to do something to banish unwanted spirits from their apartment, would praying the rosary and reciting the Daily Office (morning and night) in their apartment for one week function similarly to performing the LBRP plus the Rose Cross ritual back to back every day that week? Or would the effects be different? How do?
5. Piggybacking on the above question; if I decide (after divining and meditating) to use ceremonial magic rather than just giving advice about the problem (guidance on this already came through to me from an earlier divination), but I’m out of practice doing anything stronger than the LBRP lately, would a working of doing the LBRP and the Rose Cross every day for a week have any dangers? Too hard, too fast?

Thanks again,

- Translucent Insipid Curmudgeon

replies.title: Re: Some Qs
replies.content:
You're most welcome!

1) TM is bunny slope stuff. It's very simple mantra meditation, using one of a list of short mantras assigned by age and gender.

2) Samples are fine. Amazon's skeevy about a lot of things but they're fairly careful to keep the samples within legal limits.

3) I'd use the question "Is (describe the working) appropriate for me to do at this time?" If I were doing it with a card deck, I'd use a simple three-card spread: the first is me, the second the working, the third the outcome.

4) Good question. Since I'm not a Christian I don't happen to know much about the spiritual effects of saying the Office and praying the rosary. Anyone else?

5) No, those are good basic rituals and should be safe for you to do.

replies.title: Re: Some Qs
replies.content: 4. Some additional thoughts, meant more for anyone's consumption: I think the primary spiritual effect of practices like the Office and the Rosary is to attune the individual believer to the Holy Trinity.  I'm reminded of a doxology that doesn't see much use in the RCC: "praise God from whom all blessings flow..."  When one worships the Holy Trinity using the traditional rites, it opens that person up to a potent source of spiritual blessings and power.  The more attuned one is to that source of spiritual power, the more one can benefit from it.  This is something that I've learned from Bishop Taumaturgo's work.  Baptism initiates a person into this source, other sacraments (like confirmation and holy orders) strengthens it, and sacramentals and devotions further develop one's connection to it.  If one is going to be a Catholic mage, for example, then it is vital to use these traditional practices to develop a strong relationship with the Holy Trinity.  One's magic becomes more effective as a result.

replies.title: Re: Some Qs
replies.content:
Thank you!

No concern that LBRP + RC daily would lead to a) low energy b) feeling withdrawn from the world c) weird mood swings d) sealing myself away from things I definitely need now (like gigs, which thankfully lately are coming)?

Thanks also to the commentariat for the input :-)

replies.title: Re: Some Qs
replies.content:
If you're going to do the LBRP regularly you need to combine it with the Middle Pillar exercise. Too much banishing without invocation can be a problem -- the LBRP is the banishing, the MP the invocation. I did those daily for twenty-five years and never had the problems you've mentioned -- quite the contrary, it was in those years that I got married and succeeded in becoming a professional writer.

The Rose+Cross ritual is best done weekly unless you're facing serious spiritual challenges, or are working up to an Adeptus-grade self-initiation.

replies.title: Re: Some Qs
replies.content:
4. In my experience, engaging in traditional Catholic practices like the Rosary, the Daily Office, other prayers and sacramentals, etc., is a very effective way of driving off malevolent or unwanted spiritual forces. The Little Office of the Blessed Virgin Mary is especially good at this. I've found this is the case with most of the traditional Marian devotions. The BVM is very maternal and open to helping her children on Earth.

I don't know if these things work for the same reasons as the LBRP and the Rose Cross, though. And I will add this caveat: use the traditional forms of these devotions, not the modernist, revised forms that were created in the wake of the Second Vatican Council. I've had very little success in using the modern forms of these devotions (with the exception of St. John Paul II's Luminous Mysteries). Since most of these devotions are prayers, I've found that using the modernist forms don't put one in solid contact with the Holy Trinity and its divine power. You tend not to get much as a result. The traditional practices, however, still have great efficacy, especially when it comes to the subject matter at hand.

replies.title: Re: Some Qs
replies.content: One of the interesting things about David Lynch is that he clearly believes in the reality of demons.  His films make a great deal more sense when you realise that he is generally depicting actual demons (e.g. Mystery Man in Lost Highway) or people possessed by demons (e.g. Frank Booth in Blue Velvet).

replies.title: Re: Some Qs
replies.content:
Former Christian & Ceremonial Magician

4) If your friend gave you permission to cleanse the house and you have a background in GD, I would interpret that as consent to use whatever tools you think are most appropriate and favor the LBRP/RC. Unless you've been a devout practitioner (and I mean daily prayer rule, not just going to church weekly), you won't have the juice to use the rosary and daily office as a banishing ritual. I'm not experienced with the rosary, but the effect seems to be more personal than environmental. Celebration of the daily office will have a similar but not identical effect to banishing on the area over time, but it isn't the focus of the rite. I think what you are looking for is a house blessing and the lesser rite of exorcism.

5) I've used both rites after shelving them for a few years without any ill effect. If it counts for anything, that is what I would do.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Set aside five minutes a day to imagine things. Start by looking at some object closely, then sit down when you can't see it and imagine it as clearly as you can. Begin with simple objects and work up to complex ones. Work with real objects to begin with, because that's how you stock your mind with the detailed perceptions you can combine into imagined ones. Once you've gotten good at that, imagine yourself taking a walk along a route you know very well. Make it a short trip to start with: down the stairs to the front door and then back up, for example. Make it longer once you can do the short trip with plenty of vivid detail.  Do this daily and you'll get a strong and vivid imagination.

replies.title: Re: Prayers to Apollo
replies.content:
One option to consider are reproductions of "Kouros" statues - these were statues of idealized adolescent boys that were popular in the Archaic period. They were often found in sanctuaries of Apollo, but there is some debate about which ones (if any) depict Apollo himself. They are "stiffer" than later Greek sculpture, as the Greeks were just re-learning sculpture from Egypt and the Levant, and they borrowed some of their highly stylized forms for a time. A quick search for "kouros small statue" turned up some reproductions on Etsy and eBay, so you might be able to find something suitable.

The Wikipedia page on them is pretty good: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kouros

comments.title: Astrological symbolism query
comments.content:
Hello JMG and all readers,

I was meditating on the outer planets and their addition to the astrological corpus with the traditional seven wandering stars. It occurred to me that the significations of them do not altogether match up with the mythic archetypes they're named after:

1. Uranus (Ouranos) was the Sky Father and in some ways might be seen as the ur-conservative or patriarchal god. Yet in the modern mind, the planet named for him is associated with dynamism, change, and technology. Seems like a 180 degree shift. The only thing I can find here to make sense of it is that Uranus was cast down by his children's generation, the Titans, led by Saturn -- which was a seismic shift after all. I also think there may be some deep myth to that, in that this is a nod to the very ancient Indo-European faith, ancestral to the Greeks, which they had long since abandoned as they moved into civilized lands.

2. Neptune was the Sea God, but seems to have taken on symbolism of imagination, illusion and chaos. I suppose this is somewhat consistent with the significations of water as an element.

3. Pluto, whose discovery is roughly contemporary with the heyday of the Jungian mindset, has less obviously Hellish overtones than I would have guessed. Instead, the micro-planet seems to be associated with the collective unconscious and also the transformations that occur when unconscious urges burst into conscious life, as Jung said they eventually do.

This has perplexed me: Is this a case of gods taking on different meanings with time to meet the convenience of new discoveries, or were these planets named deliberately for symbolism hidden in their mythic background all along?

-Deneb Algedi 777-
Tangerine Translucent Stoat

replies.title: Re: Astrological symbolism query
replies.content: The outer planets weren't named by competent astrologers with an understanding of myths, but instead by astronomers disconnected from the astroligical tradition. It's been said that mythically, it makes much better sense for Uranus to be named "Prometheus", for instance.

replies.title: Re: Astrological symbolism query
replies.content:
If I may:

1) The funny thing about Uranus is that where most planets are named after similar figures in most languages, it is not. Names range from variants of Uranus, to "Sky King", to those derived from Herschel's name. I'm inclined to thus think that it's not a coincidence that it's named after a god we don't know much about: the name doesn't actually mean anything.

3) Pluto is ruler of the underworld, which is not the same thing as hell.

replies.title: Re: Astrological symbolism query
replies.content: The names of the outermost planets, as Gullindagan said, weren't given by astrologers and they don't have much of anything to do with the energies of the planets. Uranus and Neptune don't behave the way their mythic equivalents did -- Neptune, in fact, has a distinctly feminine energy and should have been named for a goddess. Pluto's name was crowdsourced, which makes it a much better fit, but even so it misses a lot of that planet's characteristics.

They most certainly are! I looked through the available papers, and saw that there is a kind of 'Magic Monthly' where Hall answers reader questions. I found this gem among the questions, quite appropriate for these troubled times:

"QUESTION - Would you advise a man to leave civilization who cannot make spiritual progress among a corrupt and evil populace?

ANSWER - It is not possible for anyone to run away from life. There is an old Arabian fable of a man who sought to escape from the evils of ex­istence only to discover that the shadow of them pursued him to the most distant parts of the earth.

At last he learned that this shadow was his own body and that the one evil that no man can escape is himself. Philosophy does not advise truth-seekers to run away from experience, but rather, to use wis­dom to face experience more intelligently. These stirring and difficult times in which we live are important to the soul growth of each individual.

Philosophy is not merely a studying of books or a thinking of beautiful thoughts. Philosophy is living well in a world which tests the capacity of the in­dividual to live well. Life itself is an initiation into the sanctuary of the Divine Mystery. As neo­phytes of old were tested as to courage and integrity and wisdom by various trials devised by the priests, so modern truth-seekers are tested by the adversity of life.

An individual who cannot achieve where he is cannot achieve anywhere else. Achievement is an inner strength rising up secure and sufficient. Strength comes from action and adversity. The years ahead will be trying years. The steel of the human soul is tempered by the flame of suffering."

Brubaker and Phillips have some pretty great noir comics I have read, like the series Criminal and the first stand-alone story of Reckless.

comments.content:
I’ve been thinking recently a lot about deities. I was raised Protestant, and now am no longer religious, but I am initiating thru the Golden Dawn, with prayers directed at a vague “Lord of the Universe”.

But I’ve been doing lots of reading in the Old Testament (Jewish Tanach) and getting a better understanding of that deity, which at the time I now understand was not monotheistic but henotheistic. YHWH was a specific deity “who’s inheritance was the sons of Jacob” while other people groups had their own deities they served. Scholars seem to generally agree that the YHWH cult only transitioned into what might now be recognizable as Jewish monotheism after the Babylonian exile.

So I have many questions in regards to this:
1. Christians believe they worship the same God that Jews worship, though most Jews I believe would disagree. Furthermore, Muslims believe they also worship the same god as Christians and Jews, though most Christians and Jews would likely disagree. What is your take? Or these three separate deities? Had they once been a single deity, and then as humans evolved, spin-off deities were created off of the original YHWH to fashion new religions? And is the Jewish YHWH in competition with the Christian YHWH and Muslim Allah now? What story could we make of this?

2. Since I no longer identify as Christian, and the more I read of the deity described in their scriptures the more repugnant I find the morality of YHWH, I am exploring how to posture myself in prayer, and whom to direct it to. Like I said, my Golden Dawn training has me direct my adoration to a “Lord of the Universe” which I am comfortable with, but also distinctly do not associate that with YHWH any longer. And I now have a more pagan perspective, believing in the presence of all these other deities while not at this time feeling drawn to worship any particular one. But as I continue to direct my prayers to the “lord of the universe” I also wonder if this is just the vestiges of my previous monotheism that I haven’t shaken off. Do you find these perspectives reconcilable at all? A universe full of gods, and yet there still being a Higher Divinity above them all? Is prayer more effective when directed at a specific deity and mythological construct? Are there any traditions that address these dilemmas that you find helpful?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) We don't know. Seriously, we don't know. Human beings have no independent source of information about gods. It's as though all you have are rumors about somebody in a trench coat and a hat who came through town and stopped at three places. What was he up to? Was it even the same person?

2) Most polytheist pantheons have a single deity who's in some kind of supervisory position to the others. That's how I conceptualized the Lord of the Universe in the GD rituals. Each of the divine names, in turn, I saw as a particular deity. Of course your mileage may vary.
